title: Optimized R2 retroelement complexes for DNA insertion into plant genomes - Nature Biotechnology
text:
Download PDF

AbstractTraditional approaches for DNA insertion into plant genomes using Agrobacterium tumefaciens result in random integration. Newer genetic engineering methods based on nucleases, prime editors, transposases and recombinases extend capabilities but remain constrained with low efficiencies, off-target integration or limited payload size. Here we adapt the avian Taeniopygia guttata R2 protein (R2Tg) for targeted DNA insertion into plant genomes by engineering R2Tg expression cassettes and RNA payloads carrying intron-disrupted reporters, with optimized ribosomal DNA homology arms and untranslated regions. In Arabidopsis thaliana protoplasts, Nicotiana benthamiana leaves and Solanum lycopersicum seedlings, our R2Tg editor system achieves targeted insertion of full-length payloads ranging from 2.2 kb to 5 kb. In Nicotiana benthamiana leaves, integration occurs, on average, at 1 copy per genome, which is 30 times more efficient than that achieved by Cas9 homology-directed repair. This work establishes an R2Tg ribonucleoprotein platform for targeted DNA insertion into plant genomes, using a multicopy genomic safe-harbor site to enable efficient addition of multikilobase genes.

MainCrop yields are increasingly threatened by rising global temperatures, intensifying drought and the expanding range of plant diseases1. Genome engineering offers a powerful, timely strategy for enhancing climate resilience, food security and sustainable biomanufacturing2,3. The development of improved crops relies heavily on the incorporation of foreign genes and heterologous metabolic pathways into plant genomes, with most approaches using Agrobacterium tumefaciens for DNA delivery and insertion. However, the random DNA integration of Agrobacterium often causes variable gene expression because of local chromatin context and nearby regulatory elements, leads to silencing and prevents precise tagging of specific genes or loci, all of which are limitations complicating functional genomics studies and requiring extensive screening across generations to identify lines with desired, stable and heritable expression4,5,6,7.Emerging targeted insertion technologies enable controlled placement of DNA sequences at predetermined genomic loci, eliminating the risk of unintended changes in gene expression and genomic instability8. Furthermore, precise DNA insertion at defined genomic sites is essential for probing gene function and regulation and enables robust outputs in synthetic gene circuits, efficient trait stacking and accelerated development of engineered crops for agriculture and bioproduction9,10. To date, multiple strategies have been developed to achieve site-specific DNA integration in plants. Programmable nucleases, such as CRISPR–Cas9, introduce targeted double-strand breaks (DSBs), which are repaired by host machinery11,12. Although Cas9 homology-directed repair (HDR) can result in precise integration using donor templates with flanking homology arms at the DSB site13, this pathway is not preferred in most plant cells, where DNA breaks are predominantly repaired by nonhomologous end joining, often leading to low efficiency, imprecise insertions and indel generation14. Moreover, DSBs can lead to unpredictable outcomes, such as large deletions or chromosomal rearrangements15.Expanding the toolkit further, retrotransposons have shown recent promise for targeted gene insertion in eukaryotes. R2 non-LTR (long terminal repeat) retrotransposons are a class of sequence-specific mobile elements that insert into the conserved 28S ribosomal DNA (rDNA) tandem repeat loci and have so far been identified in the genomes of multicellular animals, including insects, crustaceans and nonmammalian vertebrates16,17,18. R2 proteins have been successfully repurposed as high-efficiency genome editors in mammalian systems, both at their conserved target sites and at other RNA-guided genomic loci through Cas9 fusion, leveraging their target-primed reverse transcription (TPRT) mechanism19,20,21,22. Engineered R2 retrotransposons mediate RNA-templated insertions at the native 28S rDNA locus with efficiencies up to 80% in HEK293T cells and 60% in mouse embryos and they achieve up to 40% at Cas9-directed, non-rDNA sites19,20,21.R2 retrotransposons provide a unique, DSB-free approach to targeted DNA insertion at the 28S rDNA site (referred to as the 25S rDNA site in plants). The required domains for function (DNA binding, endonuclease and reverse transcriptase (RT)) are encoded within a single open reading frame, enabling a compact editor design that can be codelivered with its RNA payload as a ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complex. Moreover, the high copy number of rDNA repeats in plants (~800 copies in Arabidopsis thaliana23, ~1,247 copies in Nicotiana benthamiana24, ~1,200 copies in Solanum lycopersicum25,26 and ~6,650 copies in wheat27) can further increase integration frequency and expression.Here, we established a plant genome editor on the basis of the R2 retrotransposon that enables targeted, high-efficiency DNA insertion into the 25S rDNA loci. This system uses an optimized R2 expression and an RNA donor carrying mCherry and RUBY retrotransposition reporters with rDNA homology arms and systematically tuned 5′ and 3′ untranslated regions (UTRs). The optimized system achieved integration and robust expression of mCherry payloads in A. thaliana protoplasts, N. benthamiana leaves and S. lycopersicum seedlings at the targeted rDNA sites. In N. benthamiana leaves, efficiencies reached 24% of transformed cells, as quantified by imaging, and one copy per genome on average, as quantified by droplet digital PCR (ddPCR), for both the 2.2-kb mCherry and 5-kb RUBY payloads. Long-read sequencing enabled chromosome-level mapping of insertion events across 25S rDNA loci, while off-target insertion analysis revealed a low off-target rate of 4%. Compared to Cas9-mediated HDR targeting the same rDNA site, R2-mediated insertion is 30-fold more efficient. Analysis of insertion junctions revealed predominantly precise 5′ and 3′ junctions and bisulfite sequencing showed that inserted payloads remain unmethylated. Together, these results establish a robust, comprehensively characterized platform for precise DNA insertion into plant genomic safe-harbor loci, supporting fundamental plant biology and biotechnology.ResultsEstablishing an R2 retrotransposon-mediated platform for targeted DNA insertion in plantsBuilding on the TPRT activity of R2 proteins in mammalian cells17,18, we investigated whether the same machinery could mediate precise DNA insertion in the plant nuclear genome. In the canonical mechanism, an R2 RNP complex composed of an R2 protein and its RNA template binds the 28S rDNA locus (25S rDNA in plants) in a manner that depends on the secondary structures of RNA sequences within the 5′ and 3′ UTRs17,18. The complex then nicks the bottom strand, exposing a 3′-hydroxyl primer, and synthesizes first-strand complementary DNA (cDNA) from its RNA template17. Subsequently, R2 nicks the top strand and initiates second-strand synthesis18, where the integrated element is flanked by the R2 5′ and 3′ UTRs (Fig. 1a).Fig. 1: Overview of R2 retrotransposon-mediated targeted DNA insertion in plants.Full size imagea, Mechanism of R2 integration at the 25S rDNA through site-specific TPRT. b, Constructs for detecting R2 protein expression in plants, with GV parts added for enhanced expression. Proteins were tagged at the N terminus with HiBiT tag for luminescence detection or 1× YPEt for fluorescence detection. All proteins were also tagged at the N terminus with BP NLS tag for nuclear localization. c, An mCherry intron-containing cassette embedded within 5′ and 3′ UTRs is spliced to generate a mature RNA template for retrotransposition, enabling phenotypic detection of integration by confocal microscopy. d, R2 system expression was performed through A. tumefaciens infiltration of N. benthamiana leaves and S. lycopersicum seedlings and PEG-mediated A. thaliana protoplast transfection. e, Integration events were evaluated by microscopy, flow cytometry, amplicon PCRs, NGS and ddPCR. LHA, left homology arm; RHA, right homology arm; SD, splice donor; SA, splice acceptor. Panels c–e partially created in BioRender; Demirer, G. https://biorender.com/7dyfnhf (2026).We deployed the R2 retrotransposon system (R2 system, in short) to leverage this DSB-free integration mechanism, combined with the high copy number24,27 and transcriptional activity28,29 of the 25S rDNA array, to achieve robust expression of integrated gene sequences in plants. Efficient TPRT requires high amounts of nuclear-localized R2 protein30. Therefore, we codon-optimized R2 open reading frames for plant expression, appended an N-terminal strong bipartite nuclear localization signal (BP NLS) and fused either a high-affinity bioluminescence tag (HiBiT) for luminescence complementation or a single-copy YPet fluorescent protein to the N terminus for quantifying and/or imaging protein expression (Fig. 1b). To further increase intracellular R2 copy number, long and short intergenic regions (LIR–SIR) together with the RepA replication–initiation protein were incorporated, generating self-replicating geminiviral (GV) replicons that boost expression31 (Fig. 1b).To enable phenotypic detection of R2-mediated insertion events, we built an RNA payload to function as a retrotransposition reporter32,33, in which either the Solanum tuberosum ST-LS1 intron34 or an A. thaliana intron35-disrupted mCherry cassette was embedded between the 5′ and 3′ UTRs of the R2 element (Fig. 1c). Following transcription and intron splicing, the resulting RNA forms a contiguous template for reverse transcription by R2 protein. Hence, precise integration through the R2 system restores intronless mCherry expression, producing a fluorescence signal that enables visualization and quantification of integration by confocal microscopy.To evaluate R2 system activity across different contexts, we introduced R2 protein and RNA payload constructs into N. benthamiana leaves and S. lycopersicum seedlings through A. tumefaciens infiltration and into A. thaliana protoplasts using polyethylene glycol (PEG)-mediated transfection (Fig. 1d). A BFP marker was included in the constructs for transformed cell count normalization. Phenotypic detection was achieved by confocal microscopy for N. benthamiana and S. lycopersicum leaves and flow cytometry for A. thaliana protoplasts. Molecular characterization of insertion junctions and copy number was performed using amplicon PCR, Sanger sequencing, high-depth next-generation sequencing (NGS), Nanopore long-read sequencing and ddPCR (Fig. 1e).R2 expression optimization and functional screening identify an active R2 system in plantsTo identify a functional R2 editor in plants, we first compared the expression profiles of four codon-optimized R2 proteins: R2Bm from Bombyx mori, R2Za from Zonotrichia albicollis, R2Tg from Taeniopygia guttata and a rationally improved variant of R2Tg, R2Tg-OPT19. These proteins were expressed in N. benthamiana leaves using Agrobacterium infiltration. All proteins were tested with and without GV replicon elements to assess the impact of expression cassette copy number. Then, 3 days after infiltration, we detected the expression of all YPet-tagged R2 proteins using confocal microscopy, where the nuclear-localized YPet signal was consistently stronger in GV versions (Fig. 2a). Luminescence quantification using a plate reader for the HiBiT-tagged set of proteins confirmed that GV parts significantly increased R2 protein levels across all variants (Fig. 2b). These results established our ability to express high levels of all tested R2 proteins in plants cells and that GV replicons are an effective strategy for increasing R2 protein levels.Fig. 2: Protein expression and function screening identify optimal R2 proteins and UTR combinations.Full size imagea, Representative confocal fluorescence microscopy images of N. benthamiana leaves expressing 1× YPet-tagged R2 proteins with or without GV replication elements. Scale bars, 100 µm. b, R2 protein expression quantified by HiBiT luminescence (n = 3 biological replicates). Source dataMammalian studies use RNA templates containing autocatalytic 5′ UTR ribozymes19. Recent studies suggest that the structure and sequence composition of the RNA template 5′ end, including upstream rDNA sequences that are typically part of this ribozyme, can influence precise anneal-mediated 5′-junction formation36. To assess whether the 5′ UTR sequences in our donor constructs enable autocleavage, we performed in vitro transcription and cleavage assays using the wild-type and minimal length Tribolium castaneum and T. guttata 5′ UTR37, each fused to 100 bp of the rDNA sequence upstream of the insertion site (Supplementary Fig. 1a). Distinct cleavage products were observed at expected band sizes, confirming that all tested UTRs have autocatalytic activity in vitro (Supplementary Fig. 1b).Next, we performed a systematic screen of R2 editor functions to identify an optimal pairing between R2 protein and donor RNA architecture. Each configuration combined one of the four R2 proteins with a donor cassette encoding the mCherry-based retrotransposition reporter and different UTR combinations (Fig. 2c). We evaluated four UTR combinations, all shown to be active in mammalian systems, derived from three species: minimal T. castaneum 5′ UTR with wild-type Geospiza fortis 3′ UTR, wild-type T. castaneum 5′ UTR with wild-type G. fortis 3′ UTR, wild-type T. guttata 5′ and 3′ UTRs and wild-type T. guttata 5′ UTR paired with a minimal T. guttata 3′ UTR19,22, generating a library of 16 R2–UTR combinations (Fig. 2c). Each all-in-one construct for the R2 editor function screen also encoded a constitutive mTagBFP2 reporter to normalize with transformed cell numbers (Fig. 2d).Then, 7 days after infiltration, we imaged N. benthamiana leaves infiltrated with constructs expressing all-in-one constructs for each of the 16 R2 protein–UTR combinations and scored mCherry fluorescence as a direct readout of successful retrotransposition into the 25S rDNA (Fig. 2e). R2Bm and R2Za proteins did not yield detectable integration activity when paired with any of the four UTR combinations in our editor screen (Supplementary Fig. 1c,d). The highest percentage of leaf cells with detectable mCherry signal was obtained with wild-type R2Tg paired with its wild-type 5′ and 3′ UTRs at 0.5% efficiency (Fig. 2e,f). Two additional R2Tg editor configurations with different UTRs also yielded successful integration; R2Tg enabled detectable integration when paired with wild-type 5′ and minimal 3′ UTRs from T. guttata (0.11%) and with wild-type T. castaneum 5′ UTR and wild-type G. fortis 3′ UTR (0.05%) (Fig. 2e,f). In contrast, no mCherry signal was observed when R2Tg was paired with the minimal T. castaneum 5′ UTR and wild-type G. fortis 3′ UTR. R2Tg-OPT exhibited minimal activity only when paired with wild-type T. guttata UTRs (Supplementary Fig. 1d). Our negative control samples, consisting only of the RNA payload without R2 protein, did not generate any mCherry signal (Fig. 2f and Supplementary Fig. 2).R2Tg system optimization through improved protein and payload expression and heat-shock treatmentThe payload comprises an mCherry coding sequence in the antisense orientation relative to the RNA payload, interrupted by a plant intron in the sense orientation, flanked by R2TG wild-type 5′ and 3′ UTRs and 100-bp homology arms corresponding to the 25S rDNA target site above (Fig. 3a). Transcription of the integrated cassette can yield an mCherry signal only if the payload transcript is precisely spliced to remove the intron before R2-mediated insertion at the target locus. Agrobacterium T-DNA insertions or leaky expression cannot generate a functional, intron-free mCherry transcript because the cassette remains intron bearing or incorrectly oriented, with the splice donor and acceptor motifs present only on the sense strand, thereby ensuring the absence of false-positive signal in downstream fluorescence readouts.Fig. 3: Higher R2 copy number and heat shock increase the number of mCherry+ cells.Full size imagea, Schematic of the R2Tg payload cassette with 100-bp homology arms. b, Schematic of the R2 editors using either one-plasmid or two-plasmid delivery approaches, an intronized R2Tg-2i setup and GV-R2Tg-1. c,d, Representative confocal microscopy images of leaves infiltrated with R2 constructs under no heat shock (c) and under heat shock (d). mTagBFP2 (cyan) is a transfection control; mCherry (magenta) reports successful R2-mediated integration. Scale bars, 100 µm. e, Percentage of mCherry+ nuclei quantified from confocal microscopy images (n = 4 biological replicates). Ordinary one-way ANOVA (two-sided): F(7, 24) = 148.7, P < 0.0001; R2 = 0.98. Statistical analysis was conducted using Tukey’s HSD post hoc test adjusted for multiple comparisons. Different letters denote statistically significant differences. Values are the mean ± s.e.m. Exact pairwise P values are provided in Supplementary Table 7. Panel a partially created in BioRender; Demirer, G. https://biorender.com/ashbg3f (2026). Source dataWe suspected that transcriptional interference38 from all cassettes in the all-in-one construct may have limited the efficiency of our initial R2 editor screen. To mitigate this effect, we split the system into two plasmids: one encoding the R2Tg protein and the other the R2Tg payload, a strategy intended to reduce transcriptional interference (Fig. 3b). We further inserted two A. thaliana introns35 into the R2Tg protein-coding sequence to increase the expression of the R2 protein. Initial efforts to express R2 proteins in N. benthamiana showed that expression could also be significantly improved by introducing GV replicons. Following these insights, we tested whether separating the R2Tg editor components into two constructs (R2Tg-2), introducing introns into the R2Tg coding sequence (R2Tg-i) and flanking the full editor system with GV replicons (GV-R2Tg) would improve targeted integration efficiency in plants (Fig. 3b).Following the same method of N. benthamiana leaf Agrobacterium infiltration and quantifying number of mCherry expressing cells from confocal images, we found that the separation of R2Tg editor components into two plasmids improved integration efficiency by 1.7-fold, while the introduction of introns into the R2Tg coding sequence combined with the two-plasmid delivery approach resulted in a 2.7-fold improvement compared to the one-plasmid delivery without introns (Fig. 3c,e and Supplementary Fig. 3a). Addition of GV elements to the complete one-plasmid R2Tg editing system led to a 3.3-fold increase in mCherry+ cells (Fig. 3c,e and Supplementary Fig. 3a), achieving the highest integration efficiency of 1.8% in this set (GV-R2Tg-1). Together, these findings showed that increasing the expression strength and copy number of the R2 editor in the cells and preventing potential transcriptional interference in the all-in-one constructs can enhance targeted integration at the 25S rDNA site.Successful applications of R2 editors in mammalian cells consistently incubate cells transformed with the editors at 37 °C (refs. 19,20,21,22). The R2 editor functional screen in N. benthamiana, however, was conducted under standard plant growth conditions of 22 °C during the light period and 20 °C during the dark period in a 16-h light, 8-h dark photoperiod. Given the high body temperatures of native organisms with avian R2 proteins, we hypothesized that the lower temperatures used in plant studies may limit the efficiency of R2-mediated TPRT and that elevating temperature through heat shock may enhance integration activity.To test this, A. tumefaciens-infiltrated plants were first maintained at 25 °C for 48 h under a 16-h light, 8-h dark cycle. Plants were then exposed to a heat-shock regimen of 25 °C during the light period and 37 °C during the dark period for an additional 4 days before analysis. Heat-shock treatment of the single-plasmid R2Tg-1 system resulted in a 1.9-fold increase in mCherry+ cells compared to the non-heat-shocked R2Tg-1 system. When applied to the GV-R2Tg-1 system, heat shock yielded a 1.27-fold improvement, corresponding to a 4.2-fold increase relative to the baseline R2Tg-1 construct, reaching up to over 2% targeted integration efficiency (Fig. 3d,e and Supplementary Fig. 3a).R2Tg editor activity is quantifiable at single-cell resolution in plant protoplastsTo quantify R2Tg-mediated integration at single-cell resolution, protoplasts were isolated from N. benthamiana leaves 4 days after Agrobacterium infiltration with the R2Tg editor in a two-plasmid format, followed by same-day flow cytometry analysis (Supplementary Figs. 3b and 14a–c). mGold2t, a transformation marker encoded on the R2Tg protein expression cassette, allowed gating for the transformed cell population before gating for mCherry+ cells. Using this two-marker gating strategy, 3.5% of mGold2t+ protoplasts were mCherry+ in cells expressing the intronized R2Tg construct paired with the mCherry payload, while untransformed controls and cells receiving the R2Tg cassette with mGold2t alone showed no detectable mCherry signal (Supplementary Fig. 3c). This result establishes protoplast flow cytometry as a robust platform for single-cell quantification of R2Tg integration efficiency.A compact RNA template with minimal-homology length and 3′ ribozyme achieves the highest integration efficiencyMolecular characterization of integration junctions at the 25S rDNA locus is critical to confirm the activity and specificity of R2Tg-mediated targeted insertion. However, the repetitive structure of rDNA tandem arrays and the presence of identical homology arms within the retrotransposition reporter generate false-positive chimeric PCR products, complicating unambiguous identification of genuine integration events39. We designed a modified mCherry reporter harboring minimal-homology arms of 33 bp upstream (R33) and 4 bp downstream (R4) relative to the 25S rDNA cleavage site (Fig. 4a). We reasoned that reducing the homology arm length would minimize PCR-generated chimera formation, thereby enabling accurate quantification of authentic integration events without compromising efficiency, consistent with observations in mammalian cells21,22.Fig. 4: Increased integration efficiency and molecular validation by minimal-homology-length RNA templates with HDV ribozyme.Full size imagea, Schematics of R33-R4 and R33-R4-HDV RNA templates and two delivery formats. b, Representative confocal microscopy images of N. benthamiana leaves infiltrated with the constructs in a. mTagBFP2 (cyan, transformation marker) and mCherry (magenta, R2-mediated integration reporter). Scale bars, 100 µm. c, Percentage of mCherry+ nuclei quantified from confocal microscopy images (n = 3 biological replicates). Ordinary one-way ANOVA (two-sided): F(10, 22) = 87.71, P < 0.0001; R2 = 0.98. Statistical analysis was conducted using Tukey’s HSD post hoc test adjusted for multiple comparisons. Different letters denote statistically significant differences. Exact pairwise P values are presented in Supplementary Table 7. Values are the mean ± s.e.m. d, Target-junction PCR on gDNA isolated from infiltrated leaves (n = 3 independent replicates with similar results). e, Representative Sanger sequencing chromatograms of 5′ and 3′ integrated junction amplicons for the R33-R4-HDV paired with intronized R2Tg (R2Tg-1i). f, ddPCR assay schematic for quantification of average insertion copy number per genome using primers and a probe specific to the integrated 3′ junction, normalized against an endogenous NbEF1α control locus. g, Average insertion copy number per genome quantified by ddPCR (n = 3 biological replicates for all conditions, except R2Tg-2i with n = 6 and R2Tg-1i with n = 9). Welch’s one-way ANOVA (two-sided); W(10, 10.28) = 192.8, P < 0.0001; R2 = 0.91. Statistical analysis was conducted using Dunnett’s T3 post hoc test adjusted for multiple comparisons. Different letters denote statistically significant differences. Exact pairwise P values are presented in Supplementary Table 7. Values are the mean ± s.e.m. Source dataWe also replaced the Cassava vein mosaic virus (CsVMV) promoter (pVmv) that drives the expression of the mCherry reporter with the stronger Cauliflower mosaic virus (CaMV) 35S promoter (p35s) (Fig. 4a). To generate a homogeneous and precise 3′ RNA end, we incorporated the hepatitis delta virus (HDV) ribozyme40 downstream of the payload. This ribozyme enables autocatalytic cleavage, resulting in precise processing of the RNA transcript and retention of the R4 sequence at the 3′ terminus of the R2Tg retrotransposition reporter. These modifications yielded two distinct RNA designs, termed the R33-R4 payload and the R33-R4-HDV payload, respectively, both of which were delivered in a one-plasmid or two-plasmid system (Fig. 4a).Confocal microscopy revealed consistent mCherry signals, indicative of successful R2-mediated integration for both payload designs when paired with the wild-type R2Tg protein or an intronized version of the protein (Fig. 4b and Supplementary Fig. 4a). Quantification of the percentage of cells expressing mCherry from confocal images revealed that the new designs have substantially improved integration. In the case of the R4 design, the percentage of mCherry+ cells increased from 0.5% to 10.4% for R2Tg-1, from 0.85% to 12.2% for R2Tg-2 and from 1.6% to 15.1% for R2Tg-2i (Fig. 4c). In the case of R4-HDV design, the percentage of mCherry+ cells reached 10.7% for R2Tg-1, 12.6% for R2Tg-1i, 20.3% for R2Tg-2 and 23.6% for R2Tg-2i (Fig. 4c). Notably, R2Tg-mediated integration was significantly increased in samples expressing the R33-R4-HDV payload compared to the R4 payload (Fig. 4b,c and Supplementary Fig. 4a). In addition, intronized R2Tg variants consistently generated the most mCherry signals. The negative control, the RT-dead mutant, did not produce any detectable fluorescence, nor did the payload without the R2 protein (Fig. 4b,c and Supplementary Fig. 4a).We then performed a more thorough quantification of mCherry+ cells, normalized to the number of transformed cells, using nuclear-localized transformation markers (mGold2t for one-plasmid system and mGold2t and mTagBFP2 for two-plasmid system) in R2Tg-1i and R2Tg-2i, along with negative controls lacking R2Tg protein or payload. These findings were consistent with earlier quantification and showed an average of 14% mCherry+ cells in R2Tg-1i and 24% in R2Tg-2i, across all transformed cells (Supplementary Fig. 5).Target-junction PCR assays corroborated imaging results at the molecular level, producing amplicons of expected sizes (425 bp for 5′ junction, 454 bp for 3′ junction) in samples with R33-R4-HDV payload (Fig. 4d). Both 5′ and 3′ junction PCR products were detected for intronized and wild-type R2Tg paired with the R33-R4-HDV payload, whereas RT-dead negative controls lacked the correct-sized bands. Sanger sequencing of junction amplicons with the intronized R2Tg (R2Tg-1i) revealed precise junction sequences that perfectly matched the expected integration architecture at both the 5′ and the 3′ ends (Fig. 4e). These results validate the fidelity of the integration events generated by the optimized R2Tg system.To quantify the integration copy number, we performed a ddPCR assay targeting the 3′ and 5′ integration junctions and normalized it against the endogenous NbEF1α locus (Fig. 4f and Supplementary Fig. 4b). ddPCR across six biological replicates showed that the R2Tg editor comprising the intronized R2Tg with the R33-R4-HDV payload in the one-plasmid delivery format (R2Tg-1i) significantly exceeds efficiencies observed with all other R2 editor configurations, reaching an average of approximately one integrated 3′ junction copy per genome, while ~90% of those copies show full-length integration events (Fig. 4g and Supplementary Fig. 4c,d). ddPCR at both junction sites further revealed that, across all R2Tg editors tested, the proportion of integrated copies containing both 3′ and 5′ junctions (full-length integration events) ranged from 73% to 87% (Supplementary Fig. 4d).Deep NGS of 3′ junction amplicons showed 72.14% of reads harboring integrated junctions exclusively in samples expressing the R33-R4-HDV payload in combination with intronized R2Tg (R2Tg-1i), while samples containing the payload alone lacked any integration-specific reads (Supplementary Fig. 4e–g). Alignment of sequencing reads confirmed high-fidelity, precise integration accompanied by a minority of indel variants comprising non-emplated adenine additions and subtle shifts in endonuclease cleavage, occurring 1–2 nt upstream of the canonical nick position, consistent with the established activity profile of R2 endonuclease (Supplementary Fig. 4h).To assess insertion site specificity, we performed tagmentation-based tag integration site sequencing (TTISS) on genomic DNA (gDNA) from N. benthamiana leaves expressing the R33-R4-HDV payload and R2Tg-1i. We found that 96% of identified genomic insertions mapped to 25S rDNA sites (Fig. 5a), demonstrating high target-site specificity in planta and comparable to values reported for R2 editors in mammalian cells21. Because PCR amplification during TTISS library preparation may affect the relative recovery of insertion reads, the observed 4% off-target rate should be interpreted as an estimate rather than an absolute frequency. Off-target insertions showed a strong sequence bias toward immediately upstream of TAG motifs (Fig. 5a), consistent with base pairing with the 3′ homology sequence of the template.Fig. 5: R2 insertion site analysis.Full size imagea, Left, TTISS reads that map to 25S sites (on-target, light gray) or other genomic locations (off-target, dark gray). Counts represent the sum of three biological replicates. Right, sequence logo of off-target insertion sites across all replicates. The dotted line indicates the insertion site and the x axis shows the corresponding 25S sequence. b, Top, alignment track comparing the two most common identical groups of 25S rDNA target-site sequences in N. benthamiana, with matches shown in gray, single-nucleotide polymorphisms in black and deletions or gaps in white. The amplicon used for sequencing spans from the 3′ end of the payload into the adjacent variable rDNA sequence (blue bar). Bottom, length distribution of full-length Nanopore reads after removal of partial and nonspecific reads. c, Percentage of reads mapped to N. benthamiana chromosomes containing R2-compatible sites. White bars indicate the theoretical insertion frequency expected for each chromosome, assuming equal targeting of all compatible sites. Gray bars indicate the observed experimental distribution; individual data points (n = 4) are overlaid as dots. Data are presented as mean values ± s.d. Error bars are omitted where reads were detected in only one replicate. d, Map of the N. benthamiana genome showing the locations of 25S sites (green) and the mapped positions of R2 insertions (pink dots). e, Percentage of full-length insertion on-target 5′ junction types determined by long-read amplicon sequencing (n = 3). Mean values are displayed above bars, with dots indicating replicates.While TTISS can distinguish insertions in the 25S rDNA from off-target events, the conserved, repetitive sequences of rDNA arrays prevent locus-specific mapping of on-target insertion sites. To resolve this, we performed Nanopore long-read deep sequencing of amplicons spanning the 3′ payload end to a variable region of rDNA adjacent to the 25S sites (Fig. 5b). By spanning the variable region, this approach enabled chromosome-level assignment of all on-target insertions, which mapped predominantly to chromosomes 6 and 19 (Fig. 5c). These results demonstrate that the R2Tg editor achieves efficient targeted integration in tomato, establishing its functionality beyond model systems.DiscussionIn this work, we established targeted DNA insertion in plants using the T. guttata R2 non-LTR retroelement protein49. The R2 RNP complex achieves precise, multikilobase DNA integration by tightly coupling sequence-specific 25S rDNA recognition, bottom-strand nicking, primer handoff and reverse transcription of an RNA donor into cDNA18. Unlike CRISPR–Cas9 HDR, which depends on host DNA repair machinery to resolve a DSB using a donor DNA template, often resulting in low insertion efficiencies, the R2 mechanism bypasses DSB induction, avoiding competing end-joining pathways and the risk of large deletions or chromosomal rearrangements15.R2 editors use RNA payloads in a similar manner to prime editing. In plants, prime editors are most effective for base substitutions and short insertions and efficiency declines as insertion length and pegRNA design complexity increase50,51,52. However, unlike prime editors, which are constrained by insertion length and guide RNA design complexity, R2 editors circumvent these constraints by directly synthesizing long cDNAs at the genomic target site during integration, leveraging the high processivity of their RT domains17,18. Another unique property of R2 editors is their targeting of the high-copy 25S rDNA locus, which embeds integrated genes in a specialized chromatin environment associated with strong transgene expression and minimal risk of disrupting essential host genes53,54,55.Here, we achieve targeted insertion in N. benthamiana, A. thaliana and S. lycopersicum. We optimized integration efficiency through engineered RNA payload architectures and enhanced R2 protein expression systems and validated high-efficiency targeted insertion by molecular detection of integrated junctions and functional reporter protein expression. Our results illuminated key determinants of integration in plants. The highest measured mCherry+ cell counts were achieved with the intronized R2Tg protein and R33-R4-HDV payload while using the two-plasmid delivery approach. In contrast, the highest integrated 3′ junction copy numbers detected, one copy per genome, were obtained with an intronized R2Tg and R33-R4-HDV payload in a one-plasmid delivery. When compared side by side using the transient N. benthamiana expression assay, the R2 editor enabled 30 times more efficient integration than achieved with Cas9 HDR for the same mCherry cargo at the same site. The R2Tg editor also enabled efficient integration of a 5-kb metabolic pathway cassette into the 25S rDNA safe-harbor locus, demonstrating that R2 retrotransposons can efficiently install complex metabolic pathways at a targeted genomic site. Interestingly, the integration copy number per genome did not decrease when the payload size increased from 2 to 5 kb. It remains to be demonstrated whether R2 editors can integrate payloads much larger than 5 kb. Additionally, the R2Tg editor achieved targeted insertion in tomato, expanding the tool’s capabilities to crop engineering.The N. benthamiana genome contains more than 1,000 25S rDNA loci with nearly identical sequences. Because the R2 editor cannot distinguish among these repeats, insertions can, in principle, occur at any of these sites. We show that 96% of recovered genomic insertions mapped to 25S rDNA sites, supporting high target-site specificity of the R2Tg editor in plants, while long-read analysis revealed no strong site preference among 25S loci. Long-read amplicon sequencing also indicates accurate 5′ junction formation between the insert and rDNA, with seamless anneal-type junctions predominating. However, differences in amplicon size between junction classes may bias their relative recovery. Therefore, orthogonal approaches, such as whole-genome sequencing, could be useful for confirming the relative abundance of these junction classes, using an approach less dependent on amplicon size and PCR product recovery.While our ddPCR measurements indicate approximately one full-length insertion per genome, this value reflects a population-level average rather than uniform single-copy integration across all cells. A potential explanation is that once an R2 molecule engages an rDNA unit, the resulting high-affinity integration complex might be sufficiently stable to create local steric or molecular exclusion56, reducing the probability of additional R2 binding or TPRT events within the same rDNA array; however, this needs to be studied in the plant rDNA context. It is also plausible that the limited R2 protein abundance and brief activity window in the transient assay, relative to nuclear volume and genome size, make a low number of insertion events per genome kinetically expected before chromatin disturbance or nucleolar organization disfavors additional events. Together, these mechanisms represent non-mutually exclusive factors that could explain, on average, a single-copy R2 insertion per cell, even in the presence of many identical target sites.We detect mCherry fluorescence in ~25% of transformed cells using the best R2Tg editor system. One possibility is that only this fraction successfully acquired the integration event, suggesting that individual cells may contain more than one transgene, given the average one-copy insertion per cell. However, it is also plausible that most transformed cells do harbor the insertion at the 25S rDNA target site but a subset does not produce functional protein products for several reasons. First, depending on which 25S rDNA repeat the mCherry cassette integrates into, that particular site may be transcriptionally inaccessible or weakly expressed because of chromatin state or local accessibility constraints. Second, some integrations may be incomplete or may contain mutations that compromise reporter function. Third, R2 may engage unprocessed mCherry transcripts before intron splicing is completed; in such cases, the resulting insertion would be detectable at the DNA level but incapable of producing functional mCherry protein. These factors could account for why fluorescence is observed in only a fraction of transformed cells, even if integration events occurred in most cells.We observed improved mCherry signal following heat-shock treatment. The increase in mCherry signal may be because of improved integration efficiency, driven by enhanced enzyme kinetics or by RNP interactions required for integration. Other temperature-sensitive host processes may also contribute to this effect, such as the impact of temperature on gene expression levels. In particular, heat stress has been shown to activate endogenous transposable elements in plants through global chromatin relaxation, reduced DNA methylation and suppression of RNA interference38,57,58,59,60. Disentangling direct catalytic effects from host epigenetic or expression changes will be important for understanding the mechanism at the molecular level.The one-plasmid system performed better overall in N. benthamiana, as assessed by average ddPCR insertion copy numbers and RUBY pigmentation accumulation upon large DNA insertion. Conversely, the two-plasmid system performed better in tomato, which uses a different transformation approach. Therefore, depending on the plant species and transformation method used, the most optimal system may differ. We recommend that researchers test both versions in their applications.The R2 editor developed in this work offers many unique advantages, along with some limitations. Key strengths include insertion into highly expressed 25S rDNA safe-harbor loci supporting robust transgene expression, higher-efficiency insertion of large DNA sequences and metabolic pathways with multiple enzymes, compared to Cas9 HDR, and no substantial silencing of the R2 machinery or inserted payload at the rDNA loci. Current limitations are that the system has so far been used only in transient assays and heritable transformations remain to be shown and R2 is not programmable to non-rDNA genomic sites, which could be achieved in the future through R2 protein–RNA engineering or fusion to programmable nucleases. Given these considerations, the present R2 platform is particularly well suited for applications that require strong expression of large genetic constructs, such as biomanufacturing, metabolic pathway reconstruction and heterologous protein production. In contrast, researchers aiming to modify endogenous plant genes with small insertions, such as fluorescent protein tags or substitutions of a few protein residues, may find programmable systems, such as Cas9 HDR or prime editing more suitable.Studies of stability and heritability will be crucial for translating this platform into breeding and crop improvement pipelines. Although we delivered the R2 system using A. tumefaciens, future efforts will focus on direct delivery of the R2 system as an RNP complex using nanoparticles61 or biolistics. This strategy would eliminate unintended T-DNA insertions, ensuring that only precise edits at the target rDNA locus are recovered. Such delivery advances will not only improve editing fidelity but also accelerate the generation of clean, marker-free lines, reduce the need for laborious screening, and broaden applicability to recalcitrant plant species. These improvements could establish R2-based editors as a practical and widely deployable platform for precise, large-fragment DNA insertion in plants, an advancement with far-reaching implications for both fundamental genome studies and crop engineering.Together, these findings establish R2 retrotransposons as a distinctive and versatile addition to the plant genome engineering toolkit. By leveraging the precision of a native retroelement and augmenting it through improved protein expression platforms and engineered RNA payload designs, this technology enables efficient, DSB-free integration of gene-sized and pathway-sized cassettes into a defined genomic safe-harbor locus in plants. With continued refinement, R2-based systems have the potential to complement and, in certain applications, surpass existing technologies for precise and targeted introduction of complex genetic traits into plant genomes.MethodsPlasmid constructionCoding sequences for four R2 non-LTR retrotransposases, R2Tg (T. guttata), R2Bm (B. mori), R2Tg-opt (rationally engineered T. guttata) and R2Za (Z. albicollis), were codon-optimized for N. benthamiana and A. thaliana using GenSmart Codon Optimization (GenScript) and synthesized as double-stranded DNA fragments (Twist Bioscience). Three of the samples were biological replicates for insertion of the R33-R4-HDV payload and the fourth was a negative control transformed with the R33-R4-HDV payload but without an R2 protein cassette.Briefly, 300–500 ng of gDNA was mixed with 3–5 µl of Endura Tn5 transposase (Zymo Research) loaded with Tn5 adaptors and tagmented following manufacturer recommendations. Oxford Nanopore sequencing of the amplicons was completed by Plasmidsaurus using R10.4.1 flow cells. Raw reads were filtered using cutadapt to remove any not between 2.5 and 3.5 kb, lacking the final 177 bp of the R2 insert 3′ end or not ending with the rDNA primer-binding site (approximately 20% of all reads passed filtering). Only alignments with >98% sequence identity and coverage were considered possible sites of DNA integration. Plasmidsaurus purified the amplicons but did not size-select them before Oxford Nanopore library preparation and sequencing on R10.4.1 flow cells. Raw sequencing reads were filtered in two sequential steps using cutadapt. Reads lacking the forward primer and expected start of the 25S rDNA sequence were discarded in the first step and reads lacking the reverse primer and expected end of the insert sequence were discarded in the second. The resulting reads, therefore, span the complete 5′ junction from rDNA into the transgene.Junction classification was performed in Python using a custom pipeline. Reads containing part of the pVmV promoter sequence present in our plasmid were removed as plasmid-derived contaminants. Each remaining read was searched for the R2 5′ rDNA homology arm and start of the payload sequence; reads without these represent possible truncated insertions or PCR chimeras and were discarded. For reads passing these filters, the 100 nt immediately upstream of the homology arm was extracted as the junction window. Reads with a mean Phred quality below 30 across this window were also excluded.Junction type was then assigned as follows. Reads in which the junction window ended with the canonical rDNA insertion site sequence were classified as anneal, indicating insertion at the expected 25S rDNA target site. For the remaining reads, the junction window was aligned locally to the most common N. benthamiana 25S rDNA sequence and to the reverse complement of the transgene cDNA sequence. Reads matching rDNA were classified as join, while reads matching the transgene reverse complement were classified as snap-back. The sequences of all join and snap-back reads are listed in Supplementary Table 6.Methylation analysis through targeted bisulfite sequencingCytosine methylation at the R2 insertion site was analyzed by bisulfite sequencing following Foerster and Mittelsten Scheid with modifications65. gDNA was extracted from post-transfection protoplasts 48 h after PEG-mediated transformation using the DNeasy plant mini kit (Qiagen, 69106) according to the manufacturer’s protocol. Products were verified on a 1.5% agarose gel and only gDNA samples confirmed to carry the insertion were carried forward for bisulfite conversion.Bisulfite conversion was performed using the EpiTect bisulfite kit (Qiagen, 59104) with some modifications to improve conversion efficiency; the denaturation step at 99 °C was extended by an additional 5 min and an extra conversion incubation of 2 h at 60 °C was inserted before the final hold at 20 °C. The nonconversion control pair amplifies only unconverted template and yields a 562-bp product; the conversion control pair amplifies only fully converted template. Successful conversion was confirmed by the presence of a 562-bp product with the conversion control primers and the absence of product with the nonconversion control primers.Bisulfite PCR was performed using the EpiMark Hot Start Taq DNA polymerase kit (New England Biolabs, M0490S). Primers were designed against bisulfite-converted sequence such that cytosine residues within primer-binding sites were represented as degenerate bases to avoid methylation bias, while minimizing the total number of degenerate positions; converted and nonconverted strands were analyzed independently, as bisulfite conversion renders the two strands noncomplementary. PCR products of the expected size were verified on a 1.5% agarose gel, purified and concentrated using the QIAquick PCR and gel cleanup kit (Qiagen, 28506) and adjusted to 10 µl before submission. Reads were reverse-complemented where necessary to place all reads in the plus-strand orientation and reads that could not be assigned to either strand were discarded. Reads where more than 20% of positions were recorded as no call were excluded. Unique binary patterns were enumerated and ranked by frequency. The ten most abundant epialleles were visualized as a binary heatmap alongside a per-site methylation bar chart colored by cytosine context. All analyses were performed in Python 3 using NumPy and Matplotlib.Replicate informationAll biological replicates were performed using different plants of the same age, with leaves at the same developmental stage. Exact biological replicate information and statistical analysis methods used are provided in the figure captions. Supplementary Table 7 contains P values for all multiple-comparison tests. For quantification of fluorescence intensity or the number of positive cells from confocal microscopy images, at least eight nonoverlapping fields of view were collected per biological replicate.Reporting summaryFurther information on research design is available in the Nature Portfolio Reporting Summary linked to this article.

Data availability

All data are included in either the article or Supplementary Information. All sequencing results are accessible through the National Center for Biotechnology Information Sequence Read Archive under BioProject PRJNA1463879. Source data are provided with this paper.
Code availability

All code used in the study is available from GitHub (https://github.com/DemirerLab). GraphPad Prism version 10.3.1 and ImageJ were used to analyze all other data. Flow cytometry data were preliminarily gated in FlowReada, followed by analysis with a Python 3 pipeline.
